summary: Spolsky argues that the fundamental difference between Unix and Windows isn't technical but cultural: Unix values code useful to other programmers, while Windows values code useful to end users. He uses Eric S. Raymond's 'The Art of UNIX Programming' as a springboard to explore how these divergent values manifest in everything from command-line output conventions to documentation style. He traces the origins of each culture — Unix emerged when all computer users were programmers, while Windows was built to reach every desktop. Spolsky contends this cultural divide explains why Unix has never succeeded on the desktop, and why Apple succeeded with OS X only by rejecting Unix's programmer-centric norms. He praises Raymond's book for its deep historical insight while criticizing its occasional cultural chauvinism toward Windows.
key_points: Unix and Windows are functionally similar but culturally divergent — Unix optimizes for programmer audiences while Windows optimizes for end users
Unix conventions like silent success, textual interfaces, and terse documentation make sense for programmer-to-programmer communication but alienate non-technical users
Apple succeeded in making Unix usable for consumers (OS X) precisely because Apple's engineers came from an end-user culture and rejected Unix's programmer-centric norms
Cultural values often get mistaken for universal truths — monocultural programmers can't distinguish between genuinely bad practices and practices that simply serve a different audience
Raymond's book is excellent for understanding Unix philosophy and many universal programming principles, but weakened by ignorance of Windows culture and occasional ideological smugness
money_quotes: Unix culture values code which is useful to other programmers, while Windows culture values code which is useful to non-programmers.
This is, of course, a major simplification, but really, that's the big difference: are we programming for programmers or end users? Everything else is commentary.

Aunt Marge might be justified in observing that a program that produces no output because it succeeded cannot be distinguished from a program that produced no output because it failed badly or a program that produced no output because it misinterpreted your request.
It may never be a desktop operating system because the culture values things which prevent it.
Apple finally created Unix for Aunt Marge, but only because the engineers and managers at Apple were firmly of the end-user culture.
There are too many monocultural programmers who, like the typical American kid who never left St. Paul, Minnesota, can't quite tell the difference between a cultural value and a core human value.

The very fact that the Unix world is so full of self-righteous cultural superiority while the Windows world is more practical stems from a culture that feels itself under siege, unable to break out of the server closet and hobbyist market and onto the mainstream desktop.
key_insight: The Unix vs. Windows divide is not about technical superiority but about fundamentally different audiences — programmers vs. end users — and mistaking one culture's values for universal truths leads to bad software and pointless religious wars.
